summary: Ronacher recounts licensing mistakes around Flask’s docs, themes, and logo to show how messy open source licensing becomes outside pure code. He argues that permissive code licenses don’t map cleanly to documentation, design assets, and branding, and that the practical consequences (like Debian’s free/non-free split) force awkward compromises. He highlights how unclear or missing licenses can unintentionally make assets effectively closed source and create downstream violations. His solution is pragmatic: adjust licenses to be explicit, simple, and distribution-friendly, even if that means giving up some desired control. The conclusion is that licensing is unavoidably complex and requires more legal literacy, but in practice people rely on good faith and “friendly reminders.”
key_points: Permissive code licenses don’t translate cleanly to documentation, templates, and design, creating hidden legal ambiguities.
Docstrings and generated docs blur the boundary between code and documentation, complicating attempts to license them separately.
Non-free or restrictive licenses for logos/themes conflict with distribution policies, causing real packaging pain.
Missing or unclear licensing on assets can retroactively put users in violation despite good intentions.
Trademark is the only reliable way to protect branding in open source, but most projects end up relying on norms instead.
money_quotes: IANAL and what I write down there might be completely wrong.

Licenses from a source POV are already crazy, but when it comes to documentation texts, images and everything else involved in a modern application or even software library the whole thing becomes way more complex.
They are clearly part of the program.
It’s fun to see how such small differences can cause such an amount of troubles for all parties involved.
At that point I’m totally convinced that there is no way, besides registering a trademark, to protect your logos for an open source project.
The point is: as long there is no license, it is closed source and Debian cannot ship it.
licensing is hard, let’s go shopping.
key_insight: Licensing gets exponentially harder once you move beyond code, and practical distribution realities often force open source authors into simpler, more permissive choices than they’d prefer.
